title: Lemon drizzle cake
subtitle: Simple loaf cake with a sharp lemon-sugar crust
excerpt: A straightforward all-in-one loaf with fresh lemon zest in the batter, finished while hot with a lemon juice and sugar drizzle that seeps into the crumb and forms a crunchy crystallised crust on top. One of the most reliable of British baking standards.
sourceNotes: Lemon drizzle cake as a distinct British baking style appears in recipe collections from the late 20th century, though the combination of a citrus sponge with a sugar-syrup finish is older. The drizzle method (pouring lemon juice and sugar over a hot cake so it soaks in and crystallises on the surface) is the defining technique, distinguishing the drizzle cake from a plain lemon sponge. The formula here is the standard British loaf version: three-egg, equal-weight sponge with lemon zest in the batter and a juice-and-granulated-sugar drizzle.

content.text: is the entire point of the recipe. The cake itself is a straightforward lemon sponge; what makes it a drizzle cake is the timing. Pour the lemon and sugar mixture over the cake while it is still hot and in the tin; that heat helps the syrup soak in rather than sit on the surface. Use granulated sugar in the drizzle, not icing sugar: the crystals set firm as the cake cools.

attrs.prepNote: juice only (the same 2 lemons, zested above)

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan). Grease and fully line a 900 g loaf tin with baking paper. Beat the softened butter and caster sugar together until pale and fluffy. Add the eggs one at a time, beating well after each. Stir in the lemon zest. Fold in the flour and baking powder in two additions, adding the milk with the second addition to keep the batter loose. The batter should drop from the spoon in a slow ribbon.
Pour into the prepared tin and level the surface. Bake for 42 to 48 minutes until the top is deep gold and a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean. Begin checking at 35 minutes.

While the cake bakes, mix the lemon juice and granulated sugar together. As soon as the cake comes out of the oven, use a skewer to poke 20 to 25 holes across the top. Pour the drizzle evenly over the surface while the cake is still in the tin and still hot. Leave to cool completely in the tin before lifting out on the paper. The crust will set as the cake cools.
